defs: In Old English, a song; a strain; a canto or portion of a ballad; a passus.

records.ety: [OE. fit, fyt; cf. E. feat neat, elegant, well made, or icel. fitja to web, knit, OD. vitten to suit, square, Goth. fētjan to adorn. √77.]

defs: Adapted to an end, object, or design; suitable by nature or by art; suited by character, qualities, circumstances, education, etc.; qualified; competent; worthy.
quotes: That which ordinary men are fit for, I am qualified in.
So fit to shoot, she singled forth among her foes who first her quarry's strength should feel.

defs: Conformed to a standard of duty, propriety, or taste; convenient; meet; becoming; proper.
quotes: Is it fit to say a king, Thou art wicked?

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Fitted (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Fitting (?).]

defs: To make fit or suitable; to adapt to the purpose intended; to qualify; to put into a condition of readiness or preparation.
quotes: The time is fitted for the duty.
The very situation for which he was peculiarly fitted by nature.

defs: To bring to a required form and size; to shape aright; to adapt to a model; to adjust; -- said especially of the work of a carpenter, machinist, tailor, etc.
quotes: The carpenter . . . marketh it out with a line; he fitteth it with planes.

defs: To supply with something that is suitable or fit, or that is shaped and adjusted to the use required.
quotes: No milliner can so fit his customers with gloves.

defs: To be suitable to; to answer the requirements of; to be correctly shaped and adjusted to; as, if the coat fits you, put it on.
quotes: That's a bountiful answer that fits all questions.
That time best fits the work.
Nor fits it to prolong the feast.

defs: To be adjusted to a particular shape or size; to suit; to be adapted; as, his coat fits very well.
The quality of being fit; adjustment; adaptedness; as of dress to the person of the wearer.
The coincidence of parts that come in contact.
The part of an object upon which anything fits tightly.

records.ety: [AS. fit strife, fight; of uncertain origin. √ 77.]

quotes: Curse on that cross, quoth then the Sarazin, That keeps thy body from the bitter fit.

defs: A sudden and violent attack of a disorder; a stroke of disease, as of epilepsy or apoplexy, which produces convulsions or unconsciousness; a convulsion; a paroxysm; hence, a period of exacerbation of a disease; in general, an attack of disease; as, a fit of sickness.
quotes: And when the fit was on him, I did mark How he did shake.

defs: A mood of any kind which masters or possesses one for a time; a temporary, absorbing affection; a paroxysm; as, a fit of melancholy, of passion, or of laughter.
quotes: All fits of pleasure we balanced by an equal degree of pain.
The English, however, were on this subject prone to fits of jealously.

defs: A passing humor; a caprice; a sudden and unusual effort, activity, or motion, followed by relaxation or inaction; an impulsive and irregular action.
A darting point; a sudden emission.
quotes: A tongue of light, a fit of flame.
